intro.title: The honest English guide to Belgrade
intro.lede: Plain-English guides to visiting Belgrade — the things actually worth doing, where to stay, what to eat and drink, how to get around, and how to make a trip out of Expo 2027 (15 May–15 August). Honest, current, and written for people who don't live here.

goodToKnow.title: Good to know about Belgrade
goodToKnow.intro: The handful of facts that make a first visit easier — each links to the full guide.

items.label: Money
items.text: Serbia uses the dinar (RSD); cards are widely accepted, but keep some cash for kiosks and markets.

items.label: Getting around
items.text: City buses, trams and trolleybuses have been free for everyone since 2025.

items.label: Visas & entry
items.text: Many nationalities enter visa-free for up to 90 days — confirm your own case before you fly.

items.label: Is it safe?
items.text: Belgrade is generally safe; the real risks are pickpockets in crowds and taxi overcharging.

items.label: When to go
items.text: April–June and September–October are the sweet spot; July–August is hot and lively.

items.label: Expo 2027
items.text: Belgrade hosts a Specialised Expo from 15 May to 15 August 2027 at Surčin.

faqSection.title: Belgrade, frequently asked
faqSection.intro: Quick, honest answers to what visitors ask most — each explored in depth across the guides.

faqs.question: Is Belgrade worth visiting?
faqs.answer: Yes. Belgrade rewards you with a layered, lived-in city — a riverside fortress, distinct neighbourhoods, big nightlife and hearty food — rather than a polished postcard old town. It is affordable, walkable in the centre, and easy to manage in English.

faqs.question: Is Belgrade safe for tourists?
faqs.answer: Belgrade is generally safe, including for solo and night-time visitors taking normal precautions. The realistic risks are petty pickpocketing in crowds and taxi or exchange overcharging, not violent crime.

faqs.question: How many days do you need in Belgrade?
faqs.answer: Three days is the comfortable sweet spot — the central sights, a neighbourhood or two, and a half-day trip. One to two days still covers the walkable old-town core.

faqs.question: What is the best area to stay in Belgrade?
faqs.answer: Stari Grad, the old town, is the easiest base for a first visit, with Dorćol, Savamala and Vračar as strong alternatives depending on your budget and whether you want nightlife or quiet.

faqs.question: Is public transport free in Belgrade?
faqs.answer: Yes. Since January 2025, Belgrade's city and suburban buses, trams and trolleybuses have been free for all passengers, with no ticket required.

faqs.question: When is Expo 2027 in Belgrade?
faqs.answer: Belgrade hosts a Specialised Expo from 15 May to 15 August 2027, on a site at Surčin near Nikola Tesla Airport.

ethosSection.title: An honest, independent guide to Belgrade
ethosSection.intro: Independent, source-checked, and built to stay useful — not a content farm chasing clicks.
ethosSection.body:
BelgradeBest is a comprehensive English-language guide to Belgrade, written for foreign visitors, newcomers, and anyone trying to understand the city from the outside. It covers [things to do and what to see](/visit-belgrade), the practical side of [planning a trip](/plan-your-trip) — airport transfers, money, getting around and staying safe — the city's [food and nightlife](/food-and-nightlife), [medical tourism in Belgrade](/medical-tourism), and everything happening with [Expo 2027](/expo-2027).

Every page is built on a curated, source-checked knowledge base and written to stay useful rather than chase the news. We leave the perishable details — live prices and exact opening hours — to the official and booking sites that keep them current, and we tell you plainly when something is confirmed, only reported, or still unknown. We stay independent: when we point you toward something to book, it's because it's the genuinely useful answer, not because someone paid for the spot.
